sentences.text: [BACKGROUND NOISE]
Good morn-- good morning.
My name is Elly Gross, E-L-L-Y, G-R-O-S-S.
I was born in Simleu Silvaniei, Romania.
S-I-M-L-E-I, slash, S-I-L-V-I-E-- E-I-- I hope I spelled correctly.
She-- S-I-M-L
U-L slash, S-I-L-V-A-N-I-E-I.
I hope I spelled it this time, correctly.
I was born February 14, 1929.
My mother's name-- maiden name-- Irina Farkas; and my father's name, Eugene-- Eugene Berkovits.
Yes, I have a-- only one brother, Adalbert Berkovits.
He was born March 16, 1939.
I was-- I grew up in Simleu Silvaniei.
This--
The town was a beautiful town, surrounded by the Meses Mountain.
The town was in a valley.
And the street was beautiful-- clean and nice.
And I was raised up as a Jewish girl-- not Hasidic, but Orthodox Jew.
My parents, they kept the Shabbat.
And my father went almost every morning to pray, to the synagogue.
And my mother kept a kosher home.
Yes.
I went.
I was registered to the only Jewish parochial-- Jewish parochial school.
And I was attending there for eight years.
Yeah, this was my regular school.
It was a mixed population-- not too many Jews.
And some of them, they were more religious.
Some of them just was more moderate.
And some of them, they were conservatives.
It was a mixed.
Yes, I-- the street where I grew up, were very few Jews-- especially my age.
And I grew up with-- together with the Christian children.
We actually-- we were four girls and four boys, inseparable.
We were playing all the time, together.
Romanian and Hungarian.
There was no problem--
--until there were children-- until later on in life.
There was no problem.
We didn't talk about religion.
And--
There was nothing-- nothing-- no-- no difference.
We were-- we were just children.
We didn't know what means to be Jew, or what means to be a Christian.
Just, I know I'm not allowed to eat pork.
And that's all.
Some of them, they were Catholic, some the Reformer, they were called.
We really were a mixed group.
But I was the only Jew.
My father was a tailor, and my mother a housewife.
At the beginning, he had.
But in 1930s, the situation in Europe-- everywhere, but in Romania, too-- was very hard.
And everybody tried to squeeze as much as possible.
So my father started to work home, because there was not really work.
Yes.
It was a very high-- very big depression.
The grown up, youngsters that-- between 18 to 30, they had no work.
They--
I-- yes, I was, because I was-- until I was 10 years old, I was the only child.
And I was like, early-matured, I should say.
That it was hard, you know, money-wise.
And everybody had hard times.
In the 1939, there's the Romanian-- the Romanian government did a general drafting for the young-- the younger people.
And our city, actually, our city had to host a whole army unit.
Very strongly.
I remember even when the people who lived on our street, actually, was an-- a-- a small unit.
And the-- the soldiers, they were settled into each house-- one, two.
We had two.
Yes, they were sleeping there.
During the day, they weren't there.
But they had to come to sleep at our house.
--I was to be 10 years-- yes, about 10 years old.
Yeah, about 10 years, yes.
At that-- 19-- I'm sorry, I-- it really was wrong.
Because that was in 1938.
Because in 1939, my brother was born.
And that already came the Hungarian era.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
I haven't--
That was the-- the newspapers, and the people, they were whispering that what happening around the Europe-- especially I was in the school in 1938, I guess, it was.
A family came to the relatives.
And their daughter-- I remember the family name was Fogel.
They walked from Poland on foot.
And the gentleman was a very gigantic man.
And the girl, also was too.
But she was put in our-- in my class.
And they were there, briefly, in-- in the city, because they emigrated to Palestine, somehow.
And there he-- this man was telling to the local Jewish people, stories-- what happened in Poland.
They burned cities and towns, and all kinds of miseries.
And the--
The Germans.
I'm sorry.
The Germans occupied Poland, at the time, already.
Maybe I mix them up, the years, because--
--'38 And '39 were so close, and so washed in my mind.
And so many other things happened, that I could mix it up.
But I definitely-- I remember.
And when this man was talking about what happened in Poland, and he was walking for three, four weeks from Poland to Simleu, nobody believed him.
Everybody was thinking that he's crazy.
So finally-- when he left-- and his family-- to Palestine, everybody was relieved.
Nobody believed him.
And instead of the Jewish community listen to him, and organize, they could've saved a lot of Jews.
But nobody believed.
And the war was in Africa already, I think.
And my mother was reading the newspaper, and my father.
And they were talking, and we had no-- no life, the children.
It was very hard.
Everybody-- we didn't-- we weren't children, anymore.
We were just young adults, I should say.
We were just, we couldn't even smile.
We-- we didn't laugh.
We just ate and went to school, did our homework.
And it was--
No.
That was already, somehow, got a distant, already.
Because they had no worry.
But I have worry.
You know, I had the-- how I should say-- grew in the worrying.
We heard things, and we didn't think about.
But just, it was the pressure on every one of us.
My-- one of my cousin, in February, 1939, from Marghita, he went to-- with Balanta Jewish Organization.
And he was a young guy, 24 years old, I guess, at the time.
And he went to Israel, to Palestine at that time.
He arrived to Palestine, with hard-- he had hard times.
And he just passed away two years ago.
He leaves beautiful family.
But my aunt got so sick when he heard that the boat capsized, that she couldn't move for six months.
She was a certain kind of paralyzed.
So I heard all those things.
But I never thought that, really, anything wrong could happen, until I had my parents home with me.
I felt safe in the house.
In March, your little brother was born.
He was a beautiful angel.
I loved him a lot.
Aldabert.
I loved him, and I was like a little mother to him.
I loved to play with him.
And when he started to walk, I actually-- I started to cry.
I love him.
He was a beautiful brunette.
And he had to be very smart.
I'm sure that he would grew up, he would be smart.
But he never reached that age.
When I was studying my homework, and-- later on in Hungarian-- excuse me-- and I repeated the poems, he learned it.
And he repeated it faster.
And he learned faster than me.
But that was later on.
You know, he was only four, four and a 1/2 or five years old.
In home, we spoke Romanian and Hungarian-- but mainly Hungarian, because my mother, she didn't speak too well Hungarian-- I mean-- I'm sorry-- Romanian.
She was born in a town where the Hungarians, they were more-- lived more-- I mean more Hungarians lived.
And she grew up learning that language.
But I was raised in a city where I learned both languages.
Actually, the children, before they walk, they have to talk both languages.
Before they walk, they have to talk the both languages.
It was a big shock, because I spoke the language, but I didn't know how to write.
So we got special teachers.
Because-- when the Hungarian occupied in 1940, the town-- not the town-- the area-- the whole part of Transylvania, by the-- the Hitler's decision-- Hitler was a chancellor in Europe, and conqueror.
He-- they brought-- the Hungarian brought in the Jewish law, where the Jewish children weren't allowed to study with the Christians.
And we got special Jewish teachers from Hungarian, brought by the community to the school.
And we were learning exactly the same thing as the others learned at the Christian gymnasium.
And the language was no problem.
But the writing was a big problem, because we didn't know the Hungarian alphabet.
It's Latin letters, but it's different by the Romanian, because they have many, many words; and the O and the U of how you spell it-- long and short.
But we coped with it, and we learned.
Because we had to do it.
And children-- they, somehow, adjust easier in their learning.
In 1940, in September, the Hungarian, when they came-- oh, in 1939, because of this crowding situation what was in the city, the army and the-- somehow, the typhus broke out in Simleu.
And my father got the typhus, too.
He was taken to the hospital in Oradea.
And my mother was staying with him.
That was in early, in about in July, 1930-- 1940.
And my brother was very little-- one year and a--
--few months.
And we were-- we were taken by my mother, to my grandmother in Marghita.
And I was staying there with--
Simleu?
Marghita from Simleu is-- Marghita from Simleu is 20 kilometer.
And--
Her mother-- my mother's mother.
My father's mother perished much before.
That's the reason I have her name.
And my brother didn't want-- got very sick.
He didn't want to be with nobody-- only with me.
And in September, in 1940, when the Hungarian came with their army to occupy the part of Romania, I was actually a little bit further than-- we-- I went to the road where my grandmother lived.
And I saw the Hungarian army coming in, with big parade.
In Oradea they were telling that only 6% of the army is Jew.
The others, they were just Hungarians-- Hungarian.
And Hungary already was Jew law-- against the Jews.
Only 6% of the army was Jews.
And-- but, you know, I was just-- at the time, I was 11.
In 1940, I was 11 years old.
So I saw the Hungarians.
They were throwing flowers.
And even the Jews, they were very happy, especially who served in-- in first world war.
They were decorated.
And they thought that they are getting-- that God is coming to the town.
And everything will be very fine, because they loved the Hungarians.
Yes. many, many Jews-- they were very happy that the Hungarian came.
From the past.
From the past, because they were very happy during the Austro-Hungarian empire.
And-- but the-- the-- as soon they arrived, the-- the pressure, anyway, was in Europe before.
The pressure was harder.
And they took away the license from the Jews.
They had stores, little stores.
Or they went to the market.
There were markets where they're selling little merchandises.
They took away the licenses.
They couldn't sell, anymore.
And it was very, very far.
And that, in this time, when the Hungarian occupied, my mother and my father, they were in the hospital in Oradea.
And first time that my father heard that the Hungarian-- now is Hungary, he was upset that he thought, why he is in Hungary, because his children are in Romania?
And I don't know how long I could talk, so I'd like to make it-- No, please.
I could talk.
Because if I--
Oh, it is, OK.
And finally, in the end of the year-- about in December-- I supposed to go to school.
But in Marghita, there was no Jewish school.
So I couldn't go to school.
And finally, in the end of December, we went home-- the whole family-- to Simleu.
And then I went back to the school, which was Jewish school.
And it was already organized for the-- for my age children, to get a formal education in Hungarian-- in Hungarian, by Jewish professors, which was both from Miskolc and other Hungarian cities.
And we were-- we were just studying and growing every day.
And the-- the pressure was constantly, and the-- the very hard time, we had-- everybody.
We had to eat.
But we couldn't afford like a candy, or a child would like to have there a fruit.
I remember my mother wanted to buy for us a watermelon.
She wouldn't buy a 1/2 of a watermelon-- just bought a slice of watermelon, that the children should know what is a watermelon taste.
And in 1942-- the--
Oh, '41, oh, OK.
Sure, I can.
In 1941, just, the year passed.
I went to the next year to the following school year.
And since the language I spoke well, before, it wasn't too hard to study, because as soon as we learned the alphabet, it was no more problem-- the reading and studying.

And in 1941, and this was-- I'm unsure the year when it was-- but I remember, there was a big talking in the city that three of the-- his nieces-- a big, famous-- Pasternak-- Joe Pasternak, who was a film producer, run away from the town.
And his father was the assistant in the synagogue.
And the three of-- of his sister children, they were with me in the same school-- lower grades, but one was in my grade, with my age, in my class.
So it was another something-- talk about in the town.
And-- and--
I just heard it, because the-- the-- his sister's children, they were in the same school.
And--
Oh, sure.

Since my early age-- when I was about three years, and I went to kindergarten-- my mother, my parents, they always send me, every summer with somebody on the bus, to go to Marghita, and where was my uncle, waiting for me at the bus stop.
So every summer, I was in Marghita, until the year of 1941, I guess.
In 1941, when was the Passover coming, was new-- matzah-- was not allowed to have-- to make matzah, there was no matzah.
So my grandmother's house, the whole family came together.
And they have an oven.
And over there, we rolled the matzah, and we made matzah for Passover.
And this was in Marghita?
In Marghita.
So I was very close to Marghita, and to my grandmother.
And I should say that I know the same, the area of Marghita and the city-- I mean the little town-- as Simleu, my city, because every summer I was there.
Actually, I had more freedom in the summer on vacation, as I had during the school year, at home.
In 1942-- I have to go there, because this is my hardest years.
My father, with other Jews-- he was in-- 39 years old-- 1942-- 39 years old, yes-- 39, 40.
He was drafted to the forced labor.
They took away about, everybody.
And they were taken to forced labor, to [NON-ENGLISH] first, and then from [NON-ENGLISH].
And on foot, they were taken all the way to the Russia-- to Russia-- no food, no nothing.
And that was the last time when I saw my father.
He loved me a lot.
Brunette, tall, and very kind with me.
I was the only child, until I was 10 years old.
And--
No, everybody.
Everybody was drafted-- drafted from between the age of 18 to 55.
And this was so, so hard, that the-- the Hungarians-- they knew what they want to do.
By taking all the man who would be able to do a resistance, or to do something, they were taken away before-- much, much, before.
We didn't know what happening, at the beginning.
But now, if I think back, I know that there was a plan, they way they did it.
Because there was nobody left, and no man left in the town-- only the children and the older ones.
The Christians, they were drafted to the army, to the regular army-- but not as the Jew.
The Jew was taken-- everyone.
And we didn't even realize what that-- that-- the way happen.
They drafted by each month, a certain age or a certain group-- by addresses, by the street.
I don't know how they did it, exactly.
But that the way it happen.
And in 1942, I was 13 years old.
So my mother had a lot of harsh time-- a hard time, because she didn't know what to work.
She didn't know what to do, how to keep the children, the home-- because the-- we had-- we had two tenants.
And because we were Jews and they were Christians, they didn't have to pay-- only 1/2 a amount of the rent, which wasn't enough, for the-- even, for the tax.
Yes.
Yes, we did.
Two tenants we had in the house-- my-- my parents' house.
And since they didn't had-- they didn't have to pay the full amount of the rent, my mother had very hard time to keep the house, and to feed the two children.
So finally, she learned how to sew.
She was doing alterations and new shirts for a store.
And she kept that.
She fed us.

And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --because she had such a hard time, that she didn't know what to do, when vacation-- and on the days when I was a 1/2 a day, eventually, off-- she put me to learn with a tailor, to do ladies' garments-- I mean not a tailor-- ladies, custom-made clothes.
So I had no more vacation, and no more free time.
Whenever I had a little time, I had to go there.
The lady liked me a lot, and she taught me.
I learned how to make, by measurements, beautiful dresses-- while I wasn't at school.
At that time, I was 13 years old, in 1942-- when after my father-- so I was just very busy, all the time.
He-- my little brother went to the-- he-- Jewish-- Jewish kindergarten, with the-- for the children, where they learned how to do-- read [NON-ENGLISH].
And he was all right.
He was going by himself.
It was in the same school yard where I went to school.
And in 19-- we lived that way about two years.
1944, in the-- in about January, we had to put the-- the yellow star.
Brother.
No.
No.
We had no radio.
I don't know here who had a radio--
--at that time.
Nobody.
We had no telephone, no radio.
Seldom we communicated, and maybe--
Yes, the post office--
--work.
We communicate.
We wrote each other letters.
And this-- now and then, we went for a brief.
But we didn't go, even, because it was-- Jew was not allowed to travel.
